title: How to handle the grief of lost years spent in survival mode
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The years you can't get back 

 I sat on my kitchen floor last Tuesday. Not because I was having some spiritual awakening. Because I was too exhausted to...
htmlContent:
The years you can't get back

I sat on my kitchen floor last Tuesday. Not because I was having some spiritual awakening. Because I was too exhausted to stand up and make tea. And in that moment, on the cold tile, it hit me. A grief so sharp I could taste it. I'm 38 years old. And I've spent most of my adult life in survival mode.

Maybe you know this feeling. The one where you do the math in your head. You count the years you spent just trying to make it through the day. The jobs you hated but couldn't leave because your nervous system couldn't handle the uncertainty of a job search. The relationships that drained you because you didn't know you were allowed to have boundaries. The dreams you buried so deep you forgot they ever existed.

Here's the thing. That grief is real. And it's not a sign that you're doing recovery wrong. It's a sign that you're finally safe enough to feel it.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stored in the body. Not as a memory you can think your way out of, but as a physical sensation, a posture, a way of being in the world. And when you start to come out of survival mode, your body finally relaxes enough to let you feel what you couldn't feel before. That's when the grief shows up. Not as a problem to fix, but as a signal that something inside you is waking up.

I spent my 20s in a fog. Dissociation was my default. I couldn't tell you what I liked, what I wanted, or who I was. I was just trying to survive the next panic attack, the next flashback, the next moment when my body decided it was under attack and there was nothing I could do to stop it. And now, in my late 30s, I'm starting to feel things again. And some of those things are really, really sad.

Right? You know what I'm talking about. The sadness of realizing you never got to be a carefree kid. The anger at the people who should have protected you. The hollow ache of looking at old photos and not recognizing the person in them. That's not weakness. That's your system saying, "I'm ready to process this now. I'm ready to let this go."

The myth of catching up

One of the cruelest messages we get from the self-help world is that we can somehow make up for lost time. That if we just work hard enough, heal fast enough, our recovery enough, we'll get back those years. I call bullshit on that. And so does Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score." He spent decades studying how trauma reshapes the brain and body. And his research shows that you can't shortcut healing. You can't rush your nervous system into feeling safe before it's ready.

Look, I get the impulse. I have it too. I've spent hours scrolling through Instagram looking for the magic protocol, the perfect supplement, the one technique that will make me feel whole again. But here's what I've learned: trying to catch up is just another form of survival mode. It's your inner critic telling you that you're behind, that you're not enough, that you need to be different than you are right now.

What if instead of trying to catch up, you just let yourself be where you are? What if the grief of lost years isn't something to get over, but something to move through? A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied directly with Stephen Porges, the founder of Polyvagal Theory. And her approach isn't about pushing through or fixing yourself. It's about learning to be with your nervous system as it is, moment by moment, without judgment.

That's the work. Not catching up. Not fixing. Just being present with the grief and letting it move through you. And that takes time. More time than you probably want it to take. But here's the weird truth: when you stop trying to rush, time starts to feel different. The years you lost don't disappear, but they stop defining your future.

Stay with me here. I know this is hard. I know the temptation to just numb out or distract yourself is strong. I do it too. But the grief of lost years isn't a wall you need to climb over. It's a river you need to wade through. And on the other side, there's something you might not expect: not a replacement for what you lost, but a new relationship with the present moment.

The body remembers what the mind forgot

Janina Fisher, a leading expert on structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of us get frozen in time. When you experience trauma, especially in childhood, parts of your psyche get stuck at the age when the trauma happened. So there might be a 6-year-old part of you that's still waiting to be rescued. A 14-year-old part that's still trying to be perfect so no one gets angry. A 22-year-old part that's still bracing for the next disaster.

And when you start to heal, those parts start to wake up. And they bring with them all the grief they've been carrying for decades. That's why the sadness can feel so overwhelming. It's not just your grief. It's the grief of all the younger versions of you who never got to feel safe, loved, or seen.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He writes about the emotional flashbacks that come with complex trauma, and how grief is a natural part of the healing process. He doesn't try to cheer you up or tell you to look on the bright side. He just sits with you in the reality of what happened and says, "This is hard. And you can get through it."

I've learned that my body needs to grieve in its own way. Sometimes that means crying until I can't breathe. Sometimes it means sitting still and feeling the weight in my chest. Sometimes it means screaming into a pillow or hitting a mattress with a foam bat. The point isn't to perform grief in a way that looks acceptable. The point is to let the energy of grief move through your body so it doesn't get stuck.

Think about it. When you suppress grief, where does it go? Into your shoulders. Into your jaw. Into your gut. Into the chronic tension that keeps you in a low-level state of fight or flight. The grief of lost years isn't just an emotion. It's a physical experience. And your body needs to process it physically.

The grief is not the enemy

I used to think that if I felt grief, it meant I was broken. That I hadn't healed enough. That I was doing something wrong. But now I see it differently. The grief is a sign that my system trusts me enough to let me feel what I couldn't feel before. It's a sign of progress, not failure.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name the stages of trauma recovery: safety, remembrance and mourning, and reconnection. Notice that mourning is right there in the middle. It's not something you skip. It's not something you rush through. It's an essential part of the process.

So how do you actually handle the grief of lost years? Here's what I've found helpful, and I offer it not as a prescription but as a possibility:

First, stop telling yourself you should be over it by now. That's your inner critic talking, and it's lying to you. Grief doesn't follow a timeline. It doesn't care about your productivity goals or your recovery milestones. It shows up when it shows up, and it leaves when it's ready.

Second, find ways to let the grief move. Not think about it. Not analyze it. Move it. Dance, stretch, shake, cry, scream. Whatever your body needs to release the stored energy of all those years of holding on.

Third, let yourself be witnessed. Find a therapist, a support group, a trusted friend who can sit with you in the hard stuff without trying to fix it. The grief of lost years is too big to carry alone. You need someone who can hold the weight with you, even if just for a moment.

Fourth, practice being in the present moment. Not as a way to escape the past, but as a way to remind yourself that you're here now. You survived. You made it. And the years you have left don't have to look like the years you lost.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson. It helps you understand why you developed the survival strategies you did, and it offers a path toward relating to yourself differently. Not by fixing your parents, but by finally giving yourself the compassion you deserved all along.

What comes after the grief

I'm not going to tell you that if you just grieve enough, you'll feel better. That's toxic positivity dressed up in recovery clothes. The truth is more complicated. The grief doesn't go away completely. It changes shape. It becomes less sharp. It becomes something you can carry without it crushing you.

But here's what I've noticed in my own process. As I've let myself feel the grief of the years I lost, something else has started to emerge. A kind of tenderness toward myself that I never had before. A willingness to be gentle with my own limitations. A recognition that the person I was in survival mode did the best she could with what she had.

And from that place, the present moment starts to feel different. Not because the past is erased, but because it's no longer the only thing I can see. There's room now for curiosity. For small pleasures. For the simple experience of being alive without constantly bracing for impact.

I still have days when the grief hits me like a wave. Days when I look at my friends who seem to have their lives together and feel a pang of envy for the years they didn't lose. But I've learned to sit with that feeling too. To let it pass through me without judging myself for having it.

Wild, right? That the very thing we're afraid to feel - the grief, the sadness, the rage - might actually be the door we need to walk through to get to the other side. Not the other side of healing, because that's not a destination you ever fully arrive at. But the other side of being able to live your life without the weight of the past dictating every choice.

So if you're sitting with the grief of lost years right now, I see you. I'm sitting with you. And I'm not going to tell you it's going to be okay, because I don't know that. What I can tell you is that you're not alone in this. And that the grief you're feeling is not a sign of weakness. It's a sign that somewhere inside you, a part of you is finally ready to live.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker - A resource for understanding C-PTSD and emotional flashbacks
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) by Lindsay C. Gibson - Validates childhood experiences and offers new ways of relating to yourself
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman - A foundational text on the stages of trauma recovery

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
